invariant_definition: One of the principal *khyāti*-vāda (theories-of-erroneous-cognition) of Indian epistemology. *Anyathā-khyāti* is the Naiyāyika theory: in the rope-snake illusion, a real snake (perceived elsewhere previously, present in memory) is presented as being at the rope's location — the cognition is a real cognition of a real object, but with the spatial-relational character mistaken. The Vedāntic schools take competing positions: Advaita holds *anirvacanīya-khyāti* (the snake-cognition is of an *anirvacanīya* something); Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā holds *viparīta-khyāti*; Prābhākara holds *akhyāti*. Madhva develops a position close to *anyathā-khyāti* but with modifications. This is one of the technical Indian-epistemology issues that intersects directly with Vedāntic ontology.

per_school.definition: *Anyathā-khyāti*: erroneous cognition presents a real object (drawn from memory) as being other than it is at the present site. The snake-cognition is of a real snake, presented at the rope.

*Anirvacanīya-khyāti*: the erroneous cognition is of an *anirvacanīya* object generated by *avidyā* at the moment of cognition. Defended against *anyathā-khyāti* (Nyāya) and *akhyāti* (Prābhākara) and *ātma-khyāti* (Vijñānavāda).
*Abhinava-anyathā-khyāti* (Madhva's modification): a real object is presented at a real but mismatched site. Vyāsatīrtha defends this against the Advaita *anirvacanīya-khyāti*.

translator_note: Untranslatable in one English word. 'Theory that error is presentation-of-something-otherwise' is the standard gloss. The *khyāti*-vāda dispute is a major locus of school-divergence in Indian epistemology and is technical.
